planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Chilean guava is slow-growing and requires 2–3 years to produce a meaningful crop.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Chilean guava is a compact, evergreen shrub with small, dark-green, aromatic leaves that produces masses of tiny, deep-pink to red berries with an intensely sweet, aromatic flavor reminiscent of strawberries and vanilla — reportedly Queen Victoria's favorite jam fruit. The plant makes an excellent evergreen hedge or foundation shrub in mild climates and tolerates light frost to about 12–15°F once established. Harvest berries in fall when they are fully ripe and very dark pink-red. Excellent for jams, jellies, and fresh eating. The closely related Ugni candollei (Chilean strawberry guava) is very similar with slightly different flavors.

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil in full sun in spring; natal plum is salt-tolerant and thrives in coastal conditions, making it ideal for Gulf Coast landscapes.

directions: Root hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; natal plum is a South African native shrub with glossy, dark-green, oval leaves armed with forked spines, fragrant white flowers like large jasmine blooms, and cranberry-sized red to deep-crimson fruits that are among the finest edible berries available in zone 9–11 landscapes. The fruit has a rich, sweet, somewhat cranberry-like flavor and is excellent for jams, jellies, juices, and fresh eating. Plants bloom and fruit nearly year-round in frost-free climates. Salt-tolerant, drought-resistant, and excellent as a security hedge or coastal planting. Note: only the ripe red fruits are edible — all other plant parts including unripe fruits are toxic.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem tip cuttings in early fall or plant container-grown specimens in sharply drained, acidic, sandy or rocky, infertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; bearberry demands perfect drainage and acidic soil — it dies quickly in rich, moist, alkaline conditions.

directions: Root cuttings or plant container specimens in sharply drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy or gravelly, infertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bearberry (kinnikinnick) is a native, circumboreal evergreen groundcover that forms a tight, mat-forming carpet of small, shiny, paddle-shaped leaves with pink urn-shaped flowers in spring followed by bright red berries that ripen in fall and persist through winter, providing critical food for bears, grouse, foxes, deer, and songbirds. It is specifically adapted to barren, acid, infertile soils — adding fertilizer or organic matter kills it. Excellent for stabilizing slopes, covering dry banks under conifers, and in rock gardens. The berries are edible but mealy and very astringent when raw; historically used for medicinal tea and food in harsh conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; plant at least two shrubs for cross-pollination and better fruit set.

directions: Root softwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade, planting at least two shrubs to ensure cross-pollination; American highbush cranberry is not a true cranberry but a native viburnum that produces bright red, translucent, cranberry-sized berries in clusters from fall through winter that birds love and that can be made into preserves, sauces, and jellies with a flavor resembling true cranberries. The lacy white flower clusters in late spring are attractive, and the three-lobed leaves turn brilliant red-purple in fall. Plants are also important wildlife habitat shrubs for nesting birds. The native var. americanum is preferred over the European V. opulus (which escaped and has naturalized invasively in some areas).
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late fall or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; plant one male per three to four female plants for fruit production. Buffaloberry fixes nitrogen and improves soil fertility.

directions: Root hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, dry to average soil in full sun; silver buffaloberry is a spiny, thicket-forming native shrub of the Great Plains and Rocky Mountain West with distinctive silvery-gray foliage and clusters of brilliant orange-red to red berries that ripen in summer and fall. The plant is dioecious — male and female flowers appear on separate plants — so both sexes must be planted for fruit production. Berries are tart but edible, historically used by Plains nations to make pemmican and sauce for buffalo meat (hence the name). Plants fix nitrogen through root nodules, tolerate extreme cold, drought, alkaline soils, and salt — among the most rugged food-producing shrubs for difficult Great Plains conditions. Excellent wildlife hedge.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped specimens in well-drained, acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; mountain ash performs poorly in hot, dry climates — it is best suited to zones 3–6.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; European mountain ash is a graceful ornamental tree with pinnate, ash-like leaves that delivers three seasons of interest — clusters of creamy-white flowers in late spring, abundant bright orange-red berry clusters from late summer through winter, and orange-red fall foliage. The profuse berries are eaten by waxwings, robins, thrushes, and other songbirds so eagerly that trees can be stripped within days of peak ripeness. The berries are edible to humans but contain parasorbic acid when raw (causing nausea) and must be cooked or frozen to destroy this compound; cooked berries make excellent jelly with a tart, cranberry-like flavor. Prefers cool, moist climates and does not perform well south of zone 6.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
